title: How to Confirm Drying Goals Are Achieved — IICRC S500 §11.4 EMC Targets for Australian Restorers
description: Learn the IICRC S500:2025 standard for confirming a drying goal is achieved — EMC targets, moisture content benchmarks, and how to issue a defensible drying certificate. RestoreAssist automates drying goal validation against IICRC S500 §11.4 targets.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## What Is a Drying Goal?

A drying goal is the specific moisture content target that structural materials must reach before restoration work is considered complete and reconstruction can begin. Under IICRC S500:2025 §11.4, the restorer is responsible for establishing the drying goal at the start of the job and confirming it has been achieved with documented readings before demobilising equipment.

Failing to confirm the drying goal is one of the most common causes of mould-related callbacks — and one of the most common reasons insurance adjusters query invoices.

## Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)

The drying goal is defined in terms of equilibrium moisture content (EMC) — the moisture content a material reaches when it is in balance with its surrounding environment. EMC is not a fixed number; it varies with temperature and relative humidity.

For practical purposes, the drying goal for most structural materials in Australia is to return them to their pre-loss moisture content — or to the EMC appropriate for the prevailing climate. In temperate Australian climates, timber framing typically sits at 10–12% MC before a loss event. Plasterboard in a humidity-controlled interior typically ranges from 0.5–1.0% MC (by weight).

## Per-Material Dry Standards

IICRC S500:2025 Appendix C provides dry standard references by material type:

- **Timber framing**: Target ≤ 19% MC (structural threshold) with a goal of returning to pre-loss MC where measurable.
- **Plasterboard (gypsum board)**: Target ≤ 1% MC by weight; any reading above this warrants further monitoring.
- **Concrete slab**: Typically assessed by relative humidity probe (in-slab RH) — target ≤ 75% RH at 20 mm depth.
- **Subfloor timbers**: Target ≤ 16% MC for flooring-over-concrete applications; ≤ 19% MC for subfloor framing.
- **Hardwood flooring**: Target within 2% of pre-loss MC; confirm with control readings taken from unaffected areas of the same floor.

## Confirming the Drying Goal

Confirmation requires three elements under IICRC S500:2025 §11.4:

1. **Multiple readings** at documented grid points (minimum one reading per 9 m² of affected area)
2. **Consistent readings** — materials must achieve target MC on two consecutive daily readings
3. **Comparison to dry standard** — each reading must be compared to the material's applicable dry standard or pre-loss reference reading

All readings must be recorded with date, time, location reference (moisture map grid point), instrument type, and instrument calibration date.

## Issuing the Drying Certificate

Once all grid points have achieved target moisture content on two consecutive days, a drying certificate (also called a drying completion report or final drying report) can be issued. This document should include the initial moisture readings, the drying goal set at project initiation, the final moisture readings at each grid point, the date confirmation was achieved, and the equipment demobilisation date.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're talking about one of the most important — and most overlooked — steps in any structural drying job: confirming the drying goal is actually achieved.

A lot of technicians pull their equipment when the materials feel dry or when the dehumidifier condensate rate drops off. That's not enough. IICRC S500:2025, section 11.4, requires you to document the drying goal confirmation with moisture readings before you demobilise. Without that documentation, you're exposed to callbacks, insurance disputes, and mould liability.

So what exactly is a drying goal? It's the specific moisture content target that your structural materials need to reach before the job is done. And it's not a generic number — it depends on the material type and the pre-loss conditions.

For timber framing, the structural threshold is 19% moisture content, but your actual target should be the pre-loss moisture content for that building — typically 10 to 12% in temperate Australian climates. Plasterboard is a different beast — you're looking at 1% or less by weight. Concrete slabs are assessed by in-slab relative humidity probe, targeting 75% RH or below at 20 millimetres depth.

Hardwood floors are particularly important to handle correctly. You need to take control readings from unaffected areas of the same floor before you start, because your target is within 2% of that pre-loss reading — not a fixed number.

Now, how do you confirm the goal? IICRC S500 requires three things: readings at documented grid points — at least one per 9 square metres of affected area — those readings must hit target on two consecutive daily reads, and every reading needs to be compared against the applicable dry standard.

Once every grid point is confirmed on consecutive days, you can issue your drying certificate. This document records your initial readings, the drying goal you set at the start, the final confirmed readings at each grid point, and the date the goal was achieved.

In RestoreAssist, the drying goal validation tool does all this automatically. You set the damage category and class at the start of the job, the system sets the applicable IICRC S500 EMC targets, and as you log daily moisture readings, it tracks which grid points have passed and which haven't. When every point is confirmed, it generates the drying completion certificate ready for your insurance submission.

No more manual spreadsheets, no more missed grid points. Head to restoreassist.app to see how it works.
